coredata.dc:title: Direct solvothermal synthesis of zinc oxide nanoparticle decorated graphene oxide nanocomposite for efficient photodegradation of azo-dyes
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Atchudan, Raji
Edison, Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel
Perumal, Suguna
Shanmugam, Mani
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: Zinc oxide nanoparticles decorated graphene oxide (ZnO@GO) nanocomposite was successfully prepared using graphene oxide (GO) and zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO NPs) as raw materials by simple solvothermal method. The X-ray diffraction pattern, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic, and Raman spectroscopic techniques revealed the formation, elemental composition and the purity of ZnO NPs and ZnO@GO nanocomposite. The ZnO NPs were synthesized via simple thermal oxidation, the synthesized ZnO NPs exhibits an excellent near spherical shape with narrow size distribution and mean size of around 20±5nm which is vividly observed from field emission scanning electron microscopic images. The elemental compositions of ZnO@GO nanocomposite which carbon, oxygen and zinc were revealed by XPS and EDX elemental mapping. The ZnO NPs decorated on GO layers were clearly seen in the high resolution transmission electron microscopic images. The photocatalytic activities of the synthesized pure ZnO NPs and ZnO@GO nanocomposite were investigated by photodegradation of azo-dyes includes neutral red (NR), crystal violet (CV), congo red (CR) and methyl orange (MO) under UV-light irradiation. The results revealed that the ZnO@GO nanocomposite exhibited a remarkably higher photocatalytic efficiency compared to pure ZnO NPs. The enhancement of photocatalytic performance was ascribed to the synergistic effect between ZnO NPs and GO layers. Hence, the synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite crucial for efficient degradation of dyes such as NR, CV, CR and MO. The synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite exhibits a good photocatalytic activity along with good reproducibility of photodegradation, which is applicable for practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zinc oxide
Graphene oxide
Photocatalyst
Photodegradation
UV-light
Dye

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of pristine GO, pure ZnO NPs and ZnO@GO nanocomposite
Recycling of the synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite
Characterization of synthesized pure ZnO NPs and ZnO@GO nanocomposite
Photodegradation mechanism of the synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite
Photodegradation efficiency of synthesized pure ZnO NPs and ZnO@GO nanocomposite
Reproducibility of the synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation of ZnO@GO nanocomposites and their photodegradation mechanism under UV-light irradiation.

(a) XRD pattern, (b) FTIR spectra and (c) Raman spectra of synthesized pure ZnO NPs and ZnO@GO nanocomposite; (d) TEM images of pristine GO (inset: HRTEM image (Top) and the corresponding SAED pattern (Bottom) of pristine GO); (e)-(g) HRTEM image of ZnO@GO nanocomposite with different magnifications.
(a) FESEM image, the corresponding elemental mapping of (b) carbon, (c) oxygen, and (d) zinc, and (e) EDX spectrum (inset: atomic percentage of elements) of synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite.
(a) XPS survey scan spectrum, (b), (c) and (d) XPS high-resolution spectra of C1s, O1s and Zn 2p for synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite.

UV–vis spectra of NR degradation in the presence of (a) ZnO@GO nanocomposite and (b) pure ZnO NPs depends on irradiation time, (c) NR degradation (%) at different irradiation time and (d) first order kinetics plot of ln(At/A0) versus irradiation time for NR degradation.

UV–vis spectra of CV degradation in the presence of (a) ZnO@GO nanocomposite and (b) pure ZnO NPs depends on irradiation time, (c) CV degradation (%) at different irradiation time and (d) first order kinetics plot of ln(At/A0) versus irradiation time for CV degradation.

UV–vis spectra of CR degradation in the presence of (a) ZnO@GO nanocomposite and (b) pure ZnO NPs depends on irradiation time, (c) CR degradation (%) at different irradiation time and (d) first order kinetics plot of ln(At/A0) versus irradiation time for CR degradation.

UV–vis spectra of MO degradation in the presence of (a) ZnO@GO nanocomposite and (b) pure ZnO NPs depends on irradiation time, (c) MO degradation (%) at different irradiation time and (d) first order kinetics plot of ln(At/A0) versus irradiation time for MO degradation.

(a) Cycling effects in the photodegradation of dyes includes NR, CV, CR and MO in the presence of ZnO@GO nanocomposite under UV light irradiation; (b) XRD pattern, (c) FTIR spectrum and (d) Raman spectrum of ZnO@GO nanocomposite after 3rd cycle of photodegradation of NR.

head.ce:title: Direct solvothermal synthesis of zinc oxide nanoparticle decorated graphene oxide nanocomposite for efficient photodegradation of azo-dyes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Kyungpook National University, Daegu 41566, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Annamalai University, Annamalai Nagar, Chidambaram, Tamilnadu 608 002, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zinc oxide nanoparticles decorated graphene oxide (ZnO@GO) nanocomposite was successfully prepared using graphene oxide (GO) and zinc oxide nanoparticles (ZnO NPs) as raw materials by simple solvothermal method. The X-ray diffraction pattern, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic, and Raman spectroscopic techniques revealed the formation, elemental composition and the purity of ZnO NPs and ZnO@GO nanocomposite. The ZnO NPs were synthesized via simple thermal oxidation, the synthesized ZnO NPs exhibits an excellent near spherical shape with narrow size distribution and mean size of around 20±5nm which is vividly observed from field emission scanning electron microscopic images. The elemental compositions of ZnO@GO nanocomposite which carbon, oxygen and zinc were revealed by XPS and EDX elemental mapping. The ZnO NPs decorated on GO layers were clearly seen in the high resolution transmission electron microscopic images. The photocatalytic activities of the synthesized pure ZnO NPs and ZnO@GO nanocomposite were investigated by photodegradation of azo-dyes includes neutral red (NR), crystal violet (CV), congo red (CR) and methyl orange (MO) under UV-light irradiation. The results revealed that the ZnO@GO nanocomposite exhibited a remarkably higher photocatalytic efficiency compared to pure ZnO NPs. The enhancement of photocatalytic performance was ascribed to the synergistic effect between ZnO NPs and GO layers. Hence, the synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite crucial for efficient degradation of dyes such as NR, CV, CR and MO. The synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite exhibits a good photocatalytic activity along with good reproducibility of photodegradation, which is applicable for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Zinc oxide (ZnO) is a n-type, important semiconducting material with wide band gap (∼3.37eV) that has become a subject of intensive scientific scrutiny. It is owning a wide range of unique and fascinating properties, and was used in several applications such as nanoelectronic, optoelectronic devices and antibacterial agent, etc. In addition, due to its higher photosensitivity and wide band gap, ZnO can be considered as a promising photocatalyst alternative to TiO2 [1–4]. However, the pure ZnO exhibits high recombination rate that limit the photocatalytic applications. Therefore, reduce the recombination rate is essential for enhance the photocatalytic efficiency. In order to further enhance the photocatalytic activity and reduce the recombination rate of ZnO by linking ZnO with other materials such as noble metals, semiconductors and carbon based materials. Among them, carbon based materials especially graphene has been honeycomb lattice of sp2-bonded carbon with attracted lots of attentions by many scientific researchers due to its exceptional electrical and mechanical properties [5–11]. The graphene combined with ZnO NPs show excellent photocatalytic performance [12–21].

The organic waste outlets from the textile, paper, and plastic industries pollute the surroundings by the discharge of toxic, non-biodegradable, and carcinogenic materials. The chemicals identified as pollutants in the industrial waste water effluents must be removed before their discharge to the environment. Besides, it is essential to remove such pollutants from the ground water to achieve portable drinking water quality. In this context, photocatalytic semiconducting materials have attracted much attention in the removal of environment unfriendly organic pollutants [22–26]. Thus, the photocatalytic oxidation is an economical process, which proceeds only with non-degradable photocatalytic materials and a natural or an artificial light source. Various strategies have been adopted to synthesis photocatalytic ZnO NPs and ZnO NPs decorated graphene based nanostructured materials with controlled particle size without aggregation for better photocatalytic activity is a challengeable task [27].

In the present work, we report a synthesis of ZnO decorated graphene oxide (ZnO@GO) nanocomposite through simple solvothermal method where ZnO NPs was prepared via simple thermal oxidation and GO was synthesized via Hummers method [28]. The harvested ZnO@GO nanocomposite was thoroughly characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and Raman spectroscopy techniques in order to find the structural and elemental composition. The photocatalytic activity of the ZnO@GO nanocomposite and pure ZnO NPs was employed to decompose azo-dyes includes neutral red (NR), crystal violet (CV), congo red (CR) and methyl orange (MO) under UV-light irradiation. The photocatalytic activity was obvious that the more upstanding improvement of ZnO@GO nanocomposite than pure ZnO NPs in decomposition was attributed to the capacity of GO to accept and transfer photoelectrons (synergistic effect between ZnO NPs and GO layers).

Graphite powder (GP), zinc powder (ZnP, <50nm), NR, CV, CR and MO were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Potassium permanganate, barium chloride, hydrogen peroxide, sulfuric acid, hydrochloric acid, tetrahydrofuran, acetone, oxygen gas and hydrogen gas were purchased from Ducksan chemicals, Republic of Korea. All the chemicals were used without any further purification. The deionized (DI) water was used throughout this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of pristine GO, pure ZnO NPs and ZnO@GO nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The GO was synthesized by Hummers method, and ZnO NPs were synthesized using zinc powder by the simple thermal oxidation method with help of horizontal tubular furnace as earlier report [29]. ZnO@GO nanocomposite was synthesized using ZnO NPs and GO by a simple solvothermal method [29]. Typically, 200mg of GO was dispersed in 50mL of tetrahydrofuran (THF) in a 100mL round bottom (RB) flask. 100mg of ZnO NPs were added in to the GO dispersion and sonicated for 20min at room temperature. Subsequently, the mixture was refluxed at 65°C for 24h under static condition. The final mixture was centrifuged and washed with small amount of THF. The solid part was collected and dried at 100°C for 5h in a hot air oven. The harvesting material was collected and stored in a glass vial until further use. The schematic representation of the synthesis of pure ZnO NPs, pristine GO and ZnO@GO nanocomposite, and also the photodegradation mechanism of dyes on ZnO@GO nanocomposite was shown in Fig. 1 .

The powder XRD measurements of the samples were performed on a PANalytical X’Pert3 MRD diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ =1.54Å, 40kV and 30mA) between 10 and 90° to determine the structure of the materials. The XRD patterns were recorded in the range of 10–90° (2θ). The FTIR spectra were recorded in transmittance mode on a Perkin Elmer spectrum two in the wavenumber range of 400–4000cm−1 by the co-addition of 16 scans at a resolution of 16cm−1. Raman spectra were recorded using a Raman spectrometer Almega X/Thermo with 532nm wavelength incident laser light. XPS was performed using a K-Alpha (Thermo Scientific instrument). Casa XPS instrument software was used for the deconvolution of high resolution XPS spectrum. The surface morphologies were observed using FESEM (Hitachi S-4800) at an accelerating voltage of 4kV, and preparation of samples for the analysis, a small amount of samples was dispersed in ethanol and placing on a silicon wafer. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was recorded in the same instrument. The HRTEM images and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern were obtained using a FEI-Tecnai TF-20 operated at an acceleration voltage of 200kV. Samples for HRTEM were prepared by placing droplets of a suspension of the samples in ethanol onto a carbon-coated polymer micro grid supported on a Cu grid and dried at ambient temperature under static condition. The optical absorbance spectra of the samples was recorded using an OPTIZEN 3220UV, UV–vis spectrometer.

The photodegradation was evaluated by degradation of azo-dyes includes NR, CV, CR and MO in a neutral aqueous solution at room temperature under UV–light (λ=365nm, power 40W) irradiation. The dye solution was prepared in DI water with absorbance intensity of around 1.7 for the photodegradation experiments. In each experiment, 20mg of photocatalyst (ZnO NPs/ZnO@GO) was dispersed in 50mL of dye (NR/CV/CR/MO) aqueous solution in a 100mL glass beaker with a magnetic stirring bar, which was placed at 100mm distance from UV-light source. The suspension was irradiated under UV-light with mild stirring until the decolouration of dye. At a regular time interval of 5, 8 and 25min for NR, CV and CR/MO, respectively, 2mL of the exposed supernatant solutions was collected and was monitored using a UV–vis spectrophotometer for determination of the dye concentration. The spectra were recorded at 350–650, 400–700 and 300–600nm for NR/CR, CV and MO, respectively. The degradation efficiency (%) of NR, CV, CR and MO dyes was calculated in presence of synthesized pure ZnO NPs and ZnO@GO nanocomposite, and compared with synthesized materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recycling of the synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalyst (synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite) was recovered after the photodegradation of NR, CV, CR and MO in a neutral aqueous solution under UV–light irradiation as follows: after completion of the photodegradation reaction, the mixture was centrifuged and then residue was dried at 100°C in a hot air over under static condition. The completely dried ZnO@GO nanocomposite was successively used for the photodegradation of NR, CV, CR and MO. The photodegradation process was repeated for three cycles and then the recovered ZnO@GO nanocomposite was characterized to estimate the stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of synthesized pure ZnO NPs and ZnO@GO nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: XRD was employed to characterize the crystal structure of the synthesized materials. The wide–angle powder XRD patterns of synthesized pure ZnO NPs and ZnO@GO nanocomposite are shown in Fig. 2 (a) disclosed sharp peaks and indicated the synthesized samples were highly crystalline. All the diffraction peaks in the XRD pattern of ZnO NPs can be indexed to hexagonal phase wurtzite ZnO [30]. The peaks at 31.8, 34.5, 36.3, 47.6, 56.6, 62.9, 66.4, 68.0, 69.1, 72.6, 77.0, 81.4 and 89.6° for the (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), (201), (004), (202), (104) and (203) planes, respectively were in good agreement with the JCPDS Card no. 36-1451. The average size of ZnO NPs was 24nm, calculated using Scherrer equation based on the full width at half-maximum of the (101) diffraction plane. In the XRD pattern of ZnO@GO nanocomposite, additionally three peaks at 11.4, 26.2 and 42.5° are assigned to (002) (for graphene oxide), (002) (for reduced graphene oxide (rGO)) and (100) planes of typical graphitic structure, respectively [31]. The above observation (peak at 26.2°) shows the GO layers partially reduced to graphene layers (rGO) during the solvothermal process which is clearer from inset of Fig. 2(a). The average size of ZnO NPs within ZnO@GO nanocomposite was 21nm, calculated based on the full width at half-maximum of the (101) diffraction plane. The average size of the ZnO NPs within ZnO@GO nanocomposite was little smaller than synthesized pure ZnO NPs, this might due to splitting of some bigger nanoparticles into smaller during the solvothermal process. Moreover, the intensity of the peaks corresponding to ZnO phase decreased and broadening from pure ZnO NPs to ZnO@GO nanocomposite is clearly visible from the XRD pattern which is due to encapsulating of graphene layers on the ZnO NPs surface and size reduction ZnO NPs. Thus, the XRD results confirmed the formation of ZnO NPs over the GO layers with absence of other impurities.

FT-IR spectroscopy technique has been used extensively for the identification of functional group of the synthesized materials. Fig. 2(b) shows the FTIR spectra of the pure ZnO NPs to ZnO@GO nanocomposite. The strong and sharp absorption band below 500cm−1 corresponds to the ZnOZn stretching vibrations in ZnO structure and absence of other impurities [32]. The broad absorption band between 3050 and 3350cm−1 suggests that the OH stretching of surface hydroxyl groups and physically adsorbed water molecules [33]. The band at 1728cm−1 can be attributed to carbonyl groups (CO vibration) is clearly seen in the FTIR spectrum of synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite. The strong absorption band of the aromatic CC stretching vibration at 1575cm−1 indicates the presence of a sp2 hybridized honeycomb lattice. The absorption band at wavenumbers of 1387 and 1232cm−1 can be assigned as originating from out-of-plane bending or wagging vibration of OH (COH) and stretching vibration of CO (COH), respectively. The stretching vibrations of epoxy COC groups was exhibited at 1050cm−1. The absorption band below 500cm−1 corresponds to the ZnOZn stretching vibrations clear from spectrum of ZnO@GO nanocomposite. In addition, the absence of ZnOC bonds, confirms the absence chemical bonding between GO and ZnO NPs within ZnO@GO nanocomposite during the synthesis. Thus, FTIR spectroscopy analysis confirms that ZnO NPs physically anchored over the graphene layers. Over all, the FTIR spectra reveals that the synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite are presence of carbonyl, hydroxyl and Zn moieties on the surface of the GO layers and are well in agreement with literature work [34].

Raman spectroscopy is a one of the powerful tools and non-invasive methods to examine graphene and graphene based materials. The characteristic Raman spectrum of ZnO@GO nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 2(c). In the Raman spectra, it can be clearly seen two major peaks for the characteristic of graphene at around 1345 and 1596cm−1 known as D and G-band, respectively [35,36]. The G-bands attributed to the characteristic ordered graphitic carbon which confirmed the presence of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal graphene layer. The D-band assigned to the presence of disorder graphitic carbon [37]. In addition, ZnO peaks are faintly seen at 436 and 586cm−1, which correspond to E2 high and A1 LO, respectively. The presence of ZnO NPs was confirmed by Raman analysis which is consistent with the earlier report [38]. The intensity ratio of D to G-band was indicative of the degree of graphitization. The ID/IG intensity ratio is 1.2 which reflected the moderate degree of graphitization/moderate graphitic crystallinity [39]. The intensity of the G-band was lower than that of D-band which might due to presence of ZnO NPs and defects as well as functional groups on the surface of the GO layers.

The surface morphology of the synthesized pure ZnO NPs were examined by FESEM. Analysis of the images showed rather a dispersion of ZnO NPs with an average size of 15–25nm which is clear from Fig. S1 (see the ESI). The morphological structure of the synthesized pristine GO was examined by TEM analysis where shown in Fig. 2(d). The nature of graphene can be confirmed by the typical ripples presented on the GO layer surfaces. The TEM image reveals that the GO layers is transparent and form scrolls, and that the wrinkles are primarily located on the edge of the GO layers. Dark areas indicate the thick stacking nanostructure of several graphene oxide and/or graphene layers with some amount of oxygen functional groups. The more transparency areas indicate much thinner films of a few layers graphene oxide and/or graphene layers resulting from stacking nanostructure exfoliation. The inset shows a magnified TEM (HRTEM) image of GO layer (top) and corresponding SAED pattern (bottom). The lattice fringes are clearly seen from the HRTEM image which confirms the considerable graphitic crystallinity. The SAED shows two bright rings correspond to C(002) and C(100) planes of typical graphitic structure and good agreement with XRD results [40,41]. HRTEM images of synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite with different magnifications are shown in Fig. 2(e)–(g). The ZnO NPs were dispersed on the surface of GO layers, clear from TEM images [42,43]. The Fig. 2(f) and (g) shows the magnified view of TEM images, where ZnO NPs were randomly distributed and can be seen vividly with an average size of 20±5nm on the GO layers. In the HRTEM images, more transparently GO layer can be clearly seen which indicating layer delamination due to solvothermal synthesis of ZnO@GO nanocomposite. The intimate contact between the ZnO and the GO layers the electronic interactions between them and enhanced the photocatalytic activity.

The surface morphology and elemental composition of the synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite was revealed by FESEM with EDX elemental mapping which is shown in Fig. 3 . The low magnified FESEM image (Fig. 3a) show a spongy-like cluster morphology due to dispersion of ZnO NPs on the surface of GO layers. To further support this investigation, elemental mapping (Fig. 3b–d) of C, O and Zn and also corresponding EDX spectrum (Fig. 3e). These results suggest that the ZnO NPs are evenly dispersed on the surface of GO layers and coupled with XRD and XPS results, provides evidence for the presence of ZnO NPs on the surface of the GO layers. The atomic percentage of C, O and Zn was 66, 29 and 5% respectively (inset Fig. 3e), obtained from EDX result which indicating that the ZnO@GO nanocomposite is carbon-rich materials and good agreement with XPS results. In addition, the small peaks observed below and beyond 2keV corresponds to Si and Pt, which are originated from Si-wafer (substrate) and Pt sputtering for FESEM analysis, respectively.

In order to find the chemical composition of synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite was revealed by the XPS spectra are shown in Fig. 4 . The synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite possess predominantly carbon, oxygen and zinc on the GO layer surface which clear from the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 4(a)). The binding energy peak at ∼286eV can be attributed to the carbon bonded to functional groups (C1s), the predominant peak at ∼533eV corresponding to lattice oxygen of the ZnO NPs and/or oxygen involved to the carbon (O1s), and the Zn2p binding energy region has two peaks at a binding energy of ∼1022 and 1045eV corresponding to the Zn2p3/2 and Zn2p1/2, respectively. The peak positions of the Zn2p for the synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite was identical to the pure ZnO NPs (see the ESI Fig. S2), which indicating that the absence of chemical bonding between the ZnO NPs and GO layers. The O1s peaks of the synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite exhibit slightly higher binding energies than pure ZnO NPs, binding energy was shifted from 531 to 533eV which is clear from XPS survey spectrum. In addition, the synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite also shows higher O1s peak intensity compared to pure ZnO NPs, indicating an increased density of oxygen vacancy on the ZnO NPs surface. Oxygen vacancy could act as an electron trap that reduce the electron-hole recombination and in turn could help in effective photocatalytic degradation [44]. The high resolution of XPS spectra of C1s, O1s and Zn2p for the synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite are shown in Fig. 4(b), (c) and (d), respectively. Curve fitting of the high resolution XPS peaks can be performed using CasaXPS 2.3.16 PR1.6. Fig. 4 (b) shows the high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s level can be deconvoluted into four peaks with a binding energy of 284.7, 285.1, 286.8 and 288.2eV can be attributed to the CC/CH, CC/COC, CO/COH and OCO functional groups, respectively [45,46]. In general, C-metal peak <284eV, the synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite has absence of corresponding (COZn) binding energy peak which indicates that the no bond between C and Zn further supported by FTIR spectroscopy results [47]. The high resolution XPS spectrum of O1s level (Fig. 4(c)) located at 531.9, 532.6 and 534eV can be assigned to the ZnOZn, COH/ZnOH and CO functional groups in ZnO@GO nanocomposite, respectively. The peaks at 531.9eV are attributed to interconnection of O2− ion in the ZnO bonds and oxygen deficient defects. The peak at 532.6 is credited to the presence of lightly bounded oxygen such as adsorbed O2 and H2O on the surface of ZnO NPs [48–50]. Fig. 4(d) shows the high resolution XPS spectrum of Zn2p where deconvolution revealing two peaks at the binding energy of 1022.4 and 1045.6eV corresponding to Zn2p3/2 and Zn2p1/2 levels, respectively. The peak separation was of 23.2eV, which is in good agreement with the energy splitting of ZnO as earlier report [51]. The synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite exhibits carbon (C), oxygen (O) and zinc (Zn) with the atomic percentage of ∼65.75, 29.59 and 4.66%, respectively. Besides, the XPS results revealed that the ZnO NPs was present in the synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite and confirmed the high chemical purity of the ZnO@GO nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation mechanism of the synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para:
The synthesized pure ZnO NPs and ZnO@GO nanocomposite were used as a photocatalyst for the degradation of azo-dyes include NR, CV, CR and MO by UV-light irradiation. Considering the wide band-gap (∼3.37eV) of ZnO, photodegradation mechanism of azo-dyes under UV-light irradiation in the presence of ZnO@GO nanocomposite as illustrated in Fig. 1. In general, GO layers were large surface area with several oxygenic groups which allows good access of dyes to their surfaces in photocatalytic system. The typical photodegradation process were described as follows: The electrons of ZnO NPs are excited to generate electron–hole pairs (h+), the electrons (e−) in the valence band (VB) of ZnO are easily transferred to conduction band (CB) of GO layers under UV-light irradiation. The high separation rate of h+ makes it easier to form O2 − active ion and OH on the surface of ZnO@GO nanocomposite to interact with dyes. Concurrently, the ZnO NPs and GO layers within ZnO@GO nanocomposite might involve in photocatalytic reaction under UV-light and dye degradation occur at the same time. Such an efficient electron transfer process with GO layers as electron collector and transporter is responsible for the enhanced photocatalytic performance of the ZnO@GO nanocomposite under UV-light [52]. As for GO layer used as photocatalyst, although the efficient electron-transfer process can be occurred between active dye and GO layer, a very slow reaction rate is caused by the electron accumulation on GO surface and the rapid electron-active dye recombination [53]. Therefore, the incorporation of GO layer into ZnO NPs plays an important role to enhance the photocatalytic performance of ZnO@GO nanocomposite. The enhancement of photocatalytic performance was ascribed to the synergistic effect between ZnO NPs and GO layers. The introduction of ZnO NPs to GO layers can vastly enhance the photocatalytic performance via interfacial interaction between ZnO NPs and GO layers clear from the photodegradation results [54,55]. The plausible major photocatalytic reaction steps were summarized based on earlier reports (Eqs. (1)–(6)) [56–58].
(1) ZnO+hν→ZnO (e− (CB) +h+ (VB))→GO(e−)+h+
(2) ZnO(e−)+O2 →O2 − +ZnO
→
H
2
O
OH+ZnO
(3) ZnO(h+)+OH− → OH+ZnO
(4) GO(e−)+O2 →O2 − +GO
→
H
2
O
OH+GO
(5)
OH+O2
− +h+(VB) +Dye→degrade dye
(6)
OH+O2
− +h+(VB) +degrade dye→CO2 ↑+H2O

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation efficiency of synthesized pure ZnO NPs and ZnO@GO nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activity of synthesized pure ZnO NPs and ZnO@GO nanocomposite was studied on azo-dyes such as NR, CV, CR and MO degradation using UV–vis spectroscopy. Fig. 5 (a) shows the time dependent UV–vis spectra of NR in presence of ZnO@GO nanocomposite in a neutral aqueous solution under UV-light irradiation. The absorbance intensity of NR with ZnO@GO nanocomposite decreases with increasing the time of UV-light irradiation. The complete decolorization occurs at 20min of irradiation and the corresponding degradation efficiency equal to 100%. Similar degradation process was followed for NR in presence of ZnO NPs in a neutral aqueous solution under UV-light irradiation (Fig. 5(b)). The partially decolorized after 20min of irradiation and the corresponding degradation efficiency was 62.8% (Fig. 5(c)). The degradation of NR was analyzed by pseudo-first order kinetics according to the previous reports [59–62]. The kinetic of pure ZnO NPs and ZnO@GO nanocomposite efficiency (%) on NR was studied and the calculated rate constant (k) is 0.042min−1 (7×10−4 s−1) and 0.304min−1 (5.067×10−3 s−1), respectively. The rate constant of ZnO@GO nanocomposite was 7 times higher than that of pure ZnO NPs which is clear from Fig. 5(d). Hence, the degradation efficiency (%) of NR in presence of ZnO@GO nanocomposite was obviously enhance than the pure ZnO NPs. The CV neutral solution was irradiated under UV-light in presence of pure ZnO NPs and ZnO@GO nanocomposite and the absorbance intensity was measures every 8min until decolorizes the solution. Fig. 6 (a) and (b) shows the irradiation time dependent UV–vis absorbance spectra of CV in presence of ZnO@GO nanocomposite and pure ZnO NPs, respectively. The CV solution in presence of ZnO@GO nanocomposite was completely decolorized at the time of 80min irradiation. The degradation efficiency of ZnO@GO nanocomposite and pure ZnO NPs are 97 and 79%, respectively (Fig. 6(c)). The calculated rate constant (k) is 0.0327min−1 (5.45×10−4 s−1) and 0.0197min−1 (3.283×10−4 s−1) for CV in the presence of ZnO@GO nanocomposite and pure ZnO NPs, respectively which is clear observed in Fig. 6(d).

The time dependent UV–vis absorbance spectra of CR in presence of ZnO@GO nanocomposite and pure ZnO NPs were shown in Fig. 7 (a) and (b), respectively. After 150min irradiation, the degradation efficiency of ZnO@GO nanocomposite and pure ZnO NPs are 68 and 52%, respectively clear from Fig. 7(c). The synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite and pure ZnO NPs shows the photocatalytic efficiency with a rate constant (k) of 6.71×10−3 min−1 (1.118×10−4 s−1) and 4.93×10−3 min−1 (8.217×10−5 s−1), respectively. The synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite shows the highest photocatalytic activity on degradation of CR than pure ZnO NPs (Fig. 7(d)). Finally, the photocatalytic efficiency of the synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite and pure ZnO NPs was studied on MO degradation under UV-light irradiation. The MO shows orange color in water and strongly absorbed at 464nm in the visible region. Fig. 8 (a) and (b) shows the time dependent UV–vis absorbance spectra of MO in the presence of ZnO@GO nanocomposite and pure ZnO NPs, respectively. The degradation efficiency of ZnO@GO nanocomposite (66%) was higher than that of pure ZnO NPs (51.7%) which is clearly observed in Fig. 8(c). The rate constant (k) of the synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite and pure ZnO NPs were 6.16×10−3 min−1 (1.027×10−4 s−1) and 4.76×10−3 min−1 (7.933×10−5 s−1), respectively (Fig. 8(d)). Over all, these photocatalytic degradation results strongly suggest that the synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite exhibits the excellent photocatalytic activity on azo-dye including NR, CV, CR and MO degradation compared to pure ZnO NPs under UV-light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility of the synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The recyclability of the photocatalyst is more significant for practical applications. The synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite was examined for the photodegradation of NR, CV, CR and MO up to three cycles under UV–light irradiation. The decolouration time of NR, CV, CR and MO was noted for three cycles and compared with them. The degradation efficiency of the recovered ZnO@GO nanocomposite is nearly similar to that of as-received ZnO@GO nanocomposite even after three cycles (see the ESI Fig. S3). Fig. 9 a shows cycling effects in the photodegradation of azo-dyes including NR, CV, CR and MO in the presence of ZnO@GO nanocomposite under UV-light irradiation, the degradation efficiency for dyes decreased by ∼3% upon three recycling tests, indicating the photocatalytic activity has a good repeatability and the considerable stability of the ZnO@GO nanocomposite. Furthermore, the structural stability of the recovered ZnO@GO nanocomposite (after three cycles) was revealed through XRD, FTIR and Raman spectroscopy analyses and were shown in Fig. 9b–d, respectively. The XRD pattern, FTIR and Raman spectra of the recovered ZnO@GO nanocomposite (Fig. 9b–d) were similar as that of as-received ZnO@GO nanocomposite (Fig. 2a–c), indicating that the crystallinity, structure and degree of graphitization of the catalyst was not affected or chemically transformed to other organic compounds during photocatalytic process. These results strongly suggest that, the synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite possess a good photocatalytic activity under UV–light irradiation along with good reproducibility of photocatalytic degradation.

ZnO@GO nanocomposite was successfully synthesized by a facile solvothermal process. TEM observations revealed that dispersed ZnO NPs were anchored on the surface of the GO layers with an average size of 20±5nm. Raman spectrum demonstrated that ZnO@GO nanocomposite exhibits moderate graphitization with the ID/IG value of 1.2. The absence chemical bonding between GO and ZnO NPs within ZnO@GO nanocomposite were confirmed through XPS and FTIR spectroscopy analysis. Photodegradation efficiency of synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite was around 100, 97, 68 and 66% for NR, CV, CR and MO solution under irradiation of 20, 80, 150 and 150min, respectively. The synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite showed the excellent photocatalytic activity for the degradation of the azo-dyes including NR, CV, CR and MO solution under UV-light irradiation compared to the pure ZnO NPs. The enhanced photocatalytic performance of ZnO@GO nanocomposite can be attributed to the suppression of electron recombination and synergistic effect between ZnO NPs and GO layers. The method of synthesis was an economic, and synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite was an ideal candidate for photodegradation of environmentally harmful azo-dyes such as NR, CV, CR and MO. The synthesized ZnO@GO nanocomposite exhibits a good photocatalytic activity under UV–light irradiation besides a good reproducibility of photocatalytic degradation, which are of great significance for practical applications.

Field emission scanning electron microscopic images and XPS survey spectrum of synthesized pure ZnO NPs; recyclability study of the ZnO@GO nanocomposite on neutral red, crystal violet, congo red and methyl orange degradation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program of the Korean National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology (2012M3A7B4049675). This work was also supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea Government (MSIP) (NRF-2014R1A2A1A11052391) and Priority Research Centers Program (2014R1A6A1031189).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2017.01.021.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
